explicit_text: I enlisted in the United States Navy in November 1965 to avoid being drafted into the Army. I served for two years, mostly aboard the USS Roosevelt, an aircraft carrier. My role involved running supply for the V-2 Division, which handled catapults and arresting gear, and sometimes working on the flight deck. The Navy life was tough but manageable, and I was glad to be in the Navy rather than the Army or Marines during the Vietnam War.

Life in this branch was tough but manageable, and I was glad to be in the sea service rather than the Army or Marines during the Vietnam conflict.

explicit_text: I spent my entire two years of Navy service aboard the USS Roosevelt, an aircraft carrier based in Mayport, Florida. We were the first East Coast carrier to go to Vietnam, sailing around South Africa and stopping in places like Cuba and Brazil. My job was running supply for the V-2 Division and sometimes working on the flight deck. The Roosevelt was my home during the war, and I experienced a lot of intense moments there.

explicit_text: I served during the Vietnam War aboard the USS Roosevelt, which was the first East Coast carrier to head to the conflict zone. We left around June 1965 and arrived late July, flying missions twelve hours a day. The war was intense, and while we were on the ship, we had to maintain the aircraft and keep the flight deck operational. It was a tough time, but I was glad to be in the Navy rather than on the ground.

explicit_text: On our way to Vietnam, the USS Roosevelt sailed all the way around South Africa, stopping in Cape Town on the return trip. We were supposed to get liberty there, but due to political pressure from Senator Chuck Percy about segregation, we were not allowed to leave the ship. It was disappointing because the locals had prepared celebrations for us, but we had to stay onboard for about three or four days.

explicit_text: When the USS Roosevelt stopped in Cape Town, South Africa, on the way back from Vietnam, we were supposed to get liberty. However, due to political pressure from Senator Chuck Percy about the segregation policies in South Africa, we were not allowed to leave the ship. explicit_text: Chuck Percy was a senator from Illinois who caused a stir when the USS Roosevelt stopped in South Africa. He raised concerns about the country's segregation policies, which led to us being confined to the ship and denied liberty in Cape Town. While his intentions were to protest segregation, many sailors, including black crew members, were upset because it spoiled what could have been a good visit.

explicit_text: Shortly after I returned from Vietnam, the USS Forrestal suffered a devastating fire and almost lost the ship. The fire started on the flight deck, where planes loaded with bombs exploded in a chain reaction. John McCain was on board and escaped just in time. It was a tragic event that killed many sailors and highlighted the dangers of using old ordnance.
